bins.name: Federal jurisdiction must respect state retail authority
bins.desc: NRECA supports Commission action on large-load interconnections only if it stays within the FPA's limits. Any new rules cannot override state and local authority over retail rates and service territories, cannot mandate wheeling to ultimate consumers, and cannot become a vehicle for retail competition.
quotes: any Commission assertion of jurisdiction over large-load interconnections must conform to the terms of the jurisdiction granted the Commission by section 201 of the FPA and thus cannot impinge on the jurisdiction of state and local electric regulatory authorities over the rates, terms, and conditions of retail electric sales to such large loads.
the FPA prohibits a Commission order that is inconsistent with state law governing retail marketing areas or that requires the transmission of electric energy directly to an ultimate consumer.
federal interconnection procedures for large loads are not, and cannot be, a backdoor route to retail competition. State and local regulators determine the terms of retail direct access.

bins.name: 20 MW threshold too low; allow regional variance
bins.desc: NRECA opposes a uniform 20 MW threshold applied nationwide, arguing many cooperatives view it as too low. The Commission should let regions propose higher thresholds and vary limits based on load characteristics and local system conditions.
quotes: NRECA agrees that a lower-bound size limit on 'large loads' is necessary, but it opposes uniform, nationwide interconnection rules for all loads greater than 20 MW, which many electric cooperatives believe is too low.

the Commission should allow regional flexibility to establish a higher MW threshold, as well as different limits for different load characteristics and different transmission system configurations and conditions.

bins.name: Hybrid facility rules need breadth and flexibility
bins.desc: NRECA supports requiring protection and control facilities at hybrid sites to stop unauthorized grid injections or withdrawals, but opposes rigid study rules. Transmission providers should be able to require full reliability and market-power studies where warranted, and RTO market rules may need revision for co-location scenarios.
quotes: transmission providers should have the flexibility to establish criteria requiring full studies of the separate loss of the generation or load, and dynamic modelling of generation and load operation, where appropriate to ensure system reliability and stability.

hybrid interconnections should be required to install system protection and control facilities to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals that exceed the respective rights (e.g., leaning on the transmission system when co-located generation is not adequate to meet the large load's need).

Such control facilities are necessary not only for reliability purposes, but also to prevent market disruptions and manipulation arising from unauthorized injections or withdrawals, including the marketing of excess power unneeded to serve the hybrid load.
Additional, specific market-power studies will be necessary for these purposes in RTO/ISO regions with organized electric markets. Modifications of RTO/ISO market rules may be necessary.

bins.name: Protection facilities necessary but not sufficient
bins.desc: NRECA supports requiring system protection facilities at hybrid interconnections but notes they address only extreme events and can fail. Full reliability studies remain necessary alongside physical protection equipment.
quotes: hybrid interconnections should be required to install system protection and control facilities to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals that exceed the respective rights (e.g., leaning on the transmission system when co-located generation is not adequate to meet the large load's need).

bins.name: Large loads must pay 100% of upgrade costs
bins.desc: NRECA strongly supports the principle that interconnecting large loads pay all network upgrade costs they cause, grounded in cost-causation and beneficiary-pays doctrine. Crediting should not be a uniform requirement, and utilities need flexibility on financial security. Cooperative capital structures make absorbing these costs particularly risky for existing members.
quotes: NRECA supports this principle as essential to keep rates affordable to other consumers and to protect them from cost shifting and stranded costs.
It stands to reason that the costs resulting from the direct access of large loads to the transmission grid should be borne by those who benefit from that access.

Interconnecting loads should be responsible for the costs of these network upgrades to ensure that other transmission customers do not bear stranded transmission costs for upgrades made to accommodate the interconnection of a large load that fails to materialize, does not take service in the anticipated volume or peak and off-peak patterns, or ceases operation.

utilities should have the flexibility to determine the appropriate financial security for such upgrades. The capital structure of most cooperatives, which, by design, runs lean on the debt/equity ratio, limits their ability to absorb the costs of the build-out associated with large loads in ways that do not unfairly burden other customers.
NRECA does not support a hard-and-fast rule on whether such a crediting mechanism is appropriate for large-load interconnections.

bins.name: Cost causation: large loads pay their own way
bins.desc: NRECA backs full cost responsibility for large loads on cost-causation grounds, opposing any cost shift to existing customers. Incremental pricing for interconnection-related network upgrades is appropriate and sends efficient siting signals. Crediting rules should be case-by-case, not uniform.
quotes: NRECA supports this principle as essential to keep rates affordable to other consumers and to protect them from cost shifting and stranded costs.
It stands to reason that the costs resulting from the direct access of large loads to the transmission grid should be borne by those who benefit from that access.

Interconnecting loads should be responsible for the costs of these network upgrades to ensure that other transmission customers do not bear stranded transmission costs for upgrades made to accommodate the interconnection of a large load that fails to materialize, does not take service in the anticipated volume or peak and off-peak patterns, or ceases operation.

utilities should have the flexibility to determine the appropriate financial security for such upgrades. The capital structure of most cooperatives, which, by design, runs lean on the debt/equity ratio, limits their ability to absorb the costs of the build-out associated with large loads in ways that do not unfairly burden other customers.
NRECA does not support a hard-and-fast rule on whether such a crediting mechanism is appropriate for large-load interconnections.

bins.name: Regional flexibility over uniform rules
bins.desc: NRECA argues that regional differences in system configuration, load characteristics, and cooperative structure justify letting transmission providers set their own thresholds and study criteria rather than applying one national standard.
quotes: NRECA agrees that a lower-bound size limit on 'large loads' is necessary, but it opposes uniform, nationwide interconnection rules for all loads greater than 20 MW, which many electric cooperatives believe is too low.

the Commission should allow regional flexibility to establish a higher MW threshold, as well as different limits for different load characteristics and different transmission system configurations and conditions.

bins.name: Co-location raises reliability and market power risks
bins.desc: NRECA supports requiring control facilities at co-located sites to prevent unauthorized grid use, but warns that reliability studies alone are insufficient. Market-power studies and possible RTO rule changes are needed to address competitive risks from load co-located with existing generation.
quotes: hybrid interconnections should be required to install system protection and control facilities to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals that exceed the respective rights (e.g., leaning on the transmission system when co-located generation is not adequate to meet the large load's need).

Such control facilities are necessary not only for reliability purposes, but also to prevent market disruptions and manipulation arising from unauthorized injections or withdrawals, including the marketing of excess power unneeded to serve the hybrid load.
Additional, specific market-power studies will be necessary for these purposes in RTO/ISO regions with organized electric markets. Modifications of RTO/ISO market rules may be necessary.

bins.name: Study scope and transition need flexibility
bins.desc: NRECA opposes a rigid study requirement for hybrid facilities and calls for regional flexibility. A comprehensive transition period is also needed to coordinate new large-load rules with existing interconnection, transmission service, and planning processes.
quotes: transmission providers should have the flexibility to establish criteria requiring full studies of the separate loss of the generation or load, and dynamic modelling of generation and load operation, where appropriate to ensure system reliability and stability.

A reasonable, orderly and comprehensive transition period will be necessary. The proposed reforms, in whatever form, would require coordination with existing generator-interconnection, transmission-service request, and transmission-planning processes.

bins.name: Co-location creates market power risks
bins.desc: NRECA flags that control facilities for hybrid sites serve a market integrity purpose beyond reliability: they block unauthorized injections or withdrawals that could enable market manipulation. Dedicated market-power studies and RTO market rule changes may be necessary in organized markets.
quotes: Such control facilities are necessary not only for reliability purposes, but also to prevent market disruptions and manipulation arising from unauthorized injections or withdrawals, including the marketing of excess power unneeded to serve the hybrid load.
Additional, specific market-power studies will be necessary for these purposes in RTO/ISO regions with organized electric markets. Modifications of RTO/ISO market rules may be necessary.

bins.name: Protect existing customers from stranded upgrade costs
bins.desc: NRECA warns that if a large load fails to materialize, underperforms, or exits, existing transmission customers should not be left holding the upgrade costs. Full upfront cost responsibility from interconnecting loads is the safeguard.
quotes: Interconnecting loads should be responsible for the costs of these network upgrades to ensure that other transmission customers do not bear stranded transmission costs for upgrades made to accommodate the interconnection of a large load that fails to materialize, does not take service in the anticipated volume or peak and off-peak patterns, or ceases operation.

bins.name: Cooperative capital structure limits cost absorption
bins.desc: Cooperatives run lean debt-to-equity ratios by design, making it harder for them to absorb build-out costs for large loads without burdening existing members. NRECA uses this to support flexible financial security requirements rather than rigid upfront payment rules.
quotes: utilities should have the flexibility to determine the appropriate financial security for such upgrades. The capital structure of most cooperatives, which, by design, runs lean on the debt/equity ratio, limits their ability to absorb the costs of the build-out associated with large loads in ways that do not unfairly burden other customers.

bins.name: Coordinated transition period required
bins.desc: NRECA calls for a reasonable, orderly transition that coordinates new large-load rules with ongoing generator interconnection, transmission service, and planning processes. Regional flexibility in treating existing load interconnection studies already under way is also requested.
quotes: A reasonable, orderly and comprehensive transition period will be necessary. The proposed reforms, in whatever form, would require coordination with existing generator-interconnection, transmission-service request, and transmission-planning processes.
